items.title: What is a Terminal?
items.description: Think of Termux as a text-only way to control your phone. Instead of tapping buttons, you type commands. When you press Enter, the command runs.
items.code:
+-----------------------------------+
|  YOU TYPE:    hello                               
|  YOU PRESS:   [Enter]                             
|  COMPUTER:    "command not found"
+-----------------------------------+

# Don't worry about errors - they are just the computer saying "I don't understand that word"

items.title: Your First Command
items.description: Before installing anything, you need to update Termux's list of available apps. This is like refreshing a webpage.
items.code:
pkg update

# EXPLANATION:
# "pkg" = package manager (the app store inside Termux)
# "update" = refresh the list of available software

# After typing, press ENTER. When asked "Do you want to continue?" type Y and press ENTER.

items.title: Understanding the Prompt
items.description: The text before your cursor tells you where you are in the file system. This is called the 'prompt'.
items.code:
~ $     

# This means:
# "~" (tilde) = You are in your home folder
# "$" = Ready for your command

~/storage $ 

# This means you are now in the 'storage' folder inside your home folder.
# The prompt always tells you your current location.

items.title: The Command Pattern
items.description: Every terminal command follows a simple pattern: COMMAND followed by WHAT TO ACT ON.
items.code:
ls              # lists files
ls Documents    # lists files in Documents
cp file.txt backup/  # cp + source + destination

# Think of it like English sentences:
# "Show me" (ls) "the files" (no target needed)
# "Copy" (cp) "this file" (file.txt) "to that folder" (backup/)

items.title: Step-by-Step: Navigate Folders
items.description: Type each line and press Enter. Read the explanation before moving to the next.
items.code:
# STEP 1: Where am I?
pwd
# Shows: /data/data/com.termux/files/home

# STEP 2: What is here?
ls
# Probably nothing yet - that is fine

# STEP 3: Create a practice folder
mkdir practice

# STEP 4: Go INTO that folder
cd practice

# STEP 5: Check you moved
pwd
# Now shows: .../home/practice

# STEP 6: Go back home
cd ~
# The "~" means "home folder"

items.title: Making Your First File
items.description: Create and edit a text file - your first real interaction with the terminal.
items.code:
# Create an empty file
touch mynote.txt

# List files to see it
ls
# You should see: mynote.txt

# Edit the file (Nano is a simple editor)
nano mynote.txt

# INSIDE NANO:
# - Just type some words like "Hello Termux"
# - Press Ctrl + O (letter O, not zero) to SAVE
# - Press Enter to confirm filename
# - Press Ctrl + X to EXIT

# View what you wrote
cat mynote.txt

items.title: The Three Most Important Commands
items.description: Memorize these. You will use them constantly.
items.code:
ls  = "What is here?" - Lists all files and folders
cd  = "Go there" - Moves you into a different folder
pwd = "Where am I?" - Shows your current location

# REAL EXAMPLE:
pwd              # Shows where you are
ls               # Shows what is here
cd practice      # Go into practice folder
pwd              # Shows new location
ls               # Shows files in practice
cd ..            # Go back (.. means parent folder)

items.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items.description: Everyone makes these. Do not feel bad when you see errors.
items.code:
MISTAKE 1: Forgetting spaces
WRONG: pwdDocuments
RIGHT: pwd Documents

MISTAKE 2: Wrong capitalization
WRONG: Ls
RIGHT: ls

MISTAKE 3: Command doesn't exist
WRONG: delet file.txt
RIGHT: rm file.txt

MISTAKE 4: Wrong folder
WRONG: cat mynote.txt (file doesn't exist in current folder)
RIGHT: Use 'ls' first to see what files exist

items.title: Getting Help
items.description: When stuck, Termux has built-in help systems.
items.code:
# Most commands have a --help option
ls --help

# Or use "man" (manual pages)
pkg install man
man ls
# Press Q to exit the manual

# Command history
history
# Re-run command number 5
!5

items.title: Your First Mini Project: A Journal
items.description: Put everything together. Create a daily journal to practice what you learned.
items.code:
# 1. Create a journal folder
mkdir journal

# 2. Go into the folder
cd journal

# 3. Create today's entry
nano 2026-04-08.txt

# 4. Write your entry, then Ctrl+O to save, Ctrl+X to exit

# 5. Read your entry
cat 2026-04-08.txt

# 6. Make a backup
cp 2026-04-08.txt backup.txt

# 7. See all entries with details
ls -la

# You just used: mkdir, cd, nano, cat, cp, ls -la

items.title: What To Do Next
items.description: You have completed the beginner guide. Here is your learning path.
items.code:
# WHAT YOU LEARNED:
# - What a terminal is
# - How to read the prompt
# - Navigation: pwd, ls, cd
# - File operations: touch, nano, cat, cp
# - How to get help

# NEXT STEPS:
# Go back to the main guide and explore:
# - Getting Started
# - Basic Commands
# - Package Management

# CHALLENGE:
# Create a folder called "projects"
# Inside, create "hello.txt" with your name
# Make a copy called "hello_backup.txt"
# List both files

items.title: Where to Download Termux
items.description: The Google Play Store version is abandoned and broken. Use F-Droid version instead.
items.code:
# CORRECT SOURCE: F-Droid
https://f-droid.org/en/packages/com.termux/

# Do not use the Play Store version as it is deprecated and without updates.

# After installing, open Termux and wait. The first launch takes 10-30 seconds to set up.

items.title: First Launch and Initial Setup
items.description: When Termux first opens, you will see a black screen with white text and a $ symbol. This is normal.
items.code:
# YOUR FIRST ACTION: Update everything
pkg update
# When asked "Do you want to continue?" type Y and press Enter

pkg upgrade
# Again, type Y when asked

# This updates Termux to the latest version. Always do this first.

items.title: Grant Storage Access
items.description: Termux needs permission to see your Android files like photos, downloads, and documents.
items.code:
# Type this command:
termux-setup-storage

# Now you can access your Android files at: ~/storage/shared/

# To verify:
ls ~/storage/shared/Download/

items.title: Verify Everything Works
items.description: Run these tests to confirm your Termux installation is correct.
items.code:
# TEST 1: Check Termux version
pkg --version

# TEST 2: Check storage access
ls ~/storage/shared/

# TEST 3: Create and read a test file
echo "Termux is working" > test.txt
cat test.txt

# TEST 4: Check network connectivity
ping -c 2 google.com

# If all tests pass, your Termux is ready.

items.title: Understanding Termux Storage
items.description: Termux has its own private storage separate from Android. This is for security.
items.code:
# TERMUX PRIVATE STORAGE:
/data/data/com.termux/files/home/ # your home folder
# Other apps cannot see this folder.

# ANDROID SHARED STORAGE:
~/storage/shared/ # access to your phone's storage
# Both Termux and Android apps can access this.

# WHY THIS MATTERS:
# - Files you create go to Termux private storage by default
# - To share files with other apps, copy them to ~/storage/shared/
# - Example: 
cp myfile.txt ~/storage/shared/Documents/

items.title: Setting Up Your Environment
items.description: Optional but recommended - customize Termux for a better experience.
items.code:
# ENABLE EXTRA KEYS ROW:
echo 'extra-keys = [["ESC","CTRL","ALT","LEFT","RIGHT","UP","DOWN"]]' > ~/.termux/termux.properties
termux-reload-settings

# INSTALL A BETTER EDITOR (optional):
pkg install vim

# CHANGE COLOR SCHEME:
mkdir -p ~/.termux
nano ~/.termux/colors.properties

# Add this for a dark theme:
background=black
foreground=white

# Save and restart Termux.

items.title: pwd - Where Am I?
items.description: Shows your exact location in the file system. Use this whenever you feel lost.
items.code:
pwd  # Print Working Directory

# WHEN TO USE:
# - When you first open Termux
# - Before deleting files
# - When a command says "No such file or directory"

# EXAMPLE OUTPUT:
# /data/data/com.termux/files/home

# TIP: If you get lost, type 'cd ~' to go home, then 'pwd' to confirm.

items.title: ls - What Is Here?
items.description: Lists all files and folders in your current location.
items.code:
ls        # Basic file list (names only)
ls -la     # Shows all files in long format (including hidden ones)
ls Documents/   # List contents of a folder
ls *.txt        # List only .txt files 

# EXAMPLE OUTPUT of 'ls -la':
# drwx---- 2 user group 4096 Jan 15 10:30 .
# -rw----- 1 user group   12 Jan 15 10:30 mynote.txt
# 'd' means directory. '-' means regular file.

items.title: cd - Go There
items.description: Moves you into different folders.
items.code:
cd practice   # Go to 'practice' folder
cd ..         # Go to previous folder
cd ~          # Go to your home folder
cd /          # Go to the root of the entire file system
cd            # same as cd ~

# TIP: Tab completion - Type 'cd Docu' then press Tab - it completes to 'cd Documents/'

items.title: mkdir - Create Folders
items.description: Creates new folders to organize your files.
items.code:
mkdir projects       # Create a folder named 'projects'
mkdir -m 755 folder   # Create folder with specific permissions
mkdir folder1 folder2 folder3  # Create multiple folders at once

# BEST PRACTICES:
# - Use lowercase names (Linux is case-sensitive)
# - No spaces (use underscores or hyphens: 'my_folder' or 'my-folder')

items.title: touch - Create Empty Files
items.description: Creates an empty file or updates a file's timestamp.
items.code:
touch README.md        # Create an empty file
touch file1.txt file2.txt file3.txt  # Create multiple files
touch -t 202401151230 file.txt  # Set specific timestamp

items.title: cat - Read Files
items.description: Displays the entire contents of a file.
items.code:
cat mynote.txt      # Show everything in mynote.txt
cat file1.txt file2.txt  # Show both files one after another
cat -n filename.txt   # Show with line numbers

# LIMITATION: 'cat' shows the ENTIRE file. For long files, use 'tail' or 'head' instead.
# ALTERNATIVES:
# head mynote.txt    # Show first 10 lines
# tail mynote.txt    # Show last 10 lines

items.title: cp - Copy Files
items.description: Creates a copy of a file or folder. The original remains unchanged.
items.code:
cp source.txt destination.txt
cp -r source_folder/ dest_folder/   # Copy entire folder (recursive)
cp -p file.txt copy.txt     # Preserve permissions and timestamps

# EXAMPLE:
# cp mynote.txt ~/storage/shared/Documents/
# This copies your note to Android's Documents folder

items.title: mv - Move or Rename
items.description: Moves files to different locations OR renames them. No copy is created.
items.code:
mv oldname.txt newname.txt   # Rename file
mv file.txt ~/Documents/   # Move to a different folder
mv folder1/folder2 .    # Move a subfolder to current location

# DIFFERENCE BETWEEN CP AND MV:
# cp = COPY (original stays, new copy created)
# mv = MOVE (original moves, no copy remains)

items.title: rm - Delete Files
items.description: Permanently deletes files. There is NO undo. Be very careful.
items.code:
rm file.txt    # Delete a single file
rm -r folder/   # Delete a folder and everything inside
rm -f file.txt   # Force delete without confirmation (DANGEROUS)
rm -rf folder/   # Force delete folder without confirmation (VERY DANGEROUS)
rm *.txt     # Delete all .txt files in current directory

# SAFETY RULES:
# 1. Always use 'rm -i' when learning
# 2. Triple-check the filename
# 3. Use 'ls' first to see what you are deleting
# 4. NEVER run 'rm -rf /' - this would delete everything in Termux

items.title: echo - Print Text
items.description: Prints text to the screen. Useful for scripts and testing.
items.code:
echo 'Hello World'   # Print text
echo $HOME    # Print a variable's value
echo 'Text' > file.txt    # Write text to a file (overwrite)
echo 'More' >> file.txt      # Append text to a file

items.title: Termux Directory Structure
items.description: Termux has its own isolated file system. Here is what each important directory does.
items.code:
# YOUR HOME FOLDER:
# ~ or /data/data/com.termux/files/home
# All your personal files and projects should go here.

# PACKAGES FOLDER:
# $PREFIX or /data/data/com.termux/files/usr
# Where installed packages live. You rarely need to touch this.

# ANDROID STORAGE (after termux-setup-storage):
# ~/storage/shared/     - Your phone's internal storage
# ~/storage/downloads/  - Downloads folder
# ~/storage/dcim/       - Camera photos
# ~/storage/pictures/   - Other images
# ~/storage/music/      - Music files
# ~/storage/movies/     - Video files

items.title: Absolute vs Relative Paths
items.description: Paths tell the computer where a file is. Understanding the difference is crucial.
items.code:
# ABSOLUTE PATH: Starts from the root (/)
# Example: /data/data/com.termux/files/home/practice/mynote.txt
# This path works no matter where you are.

# RELATIVE PATH: Starts from your current location
# Example: practice/mynote.txt
# This only works if you are in the home folder.

# SPECIAL SHORTCUTS:
# .  = Current folder
# .. = Parent folder (one level up)
# ~  = Home folder
# -  = Previous folder

# EXAMPLES:
# cd /data/data/com.termux/files/home  # Absolute path (works anywhere)
# cd practice      # Relative path
# cd ../Documents     # Go up then into Documents
# cd ~/storage/shared/DCIM    # Go home then to camera photos

items.title: Viewing Files with less and head/tail
items.description: For large files, cat is not practical. Use less, head, and tail instead.
items.code:
# HEAD AND TAIL:
head -n 20 file.txt  # Show first 20 lines
tail -n 20 file.txt    # Show last 20 lines
tail -f log.txt   # Follow file as it grows (real-time)

# LESS COMMANDS (while viewing):
# less file.txt
# q = Quit
# Space = Next page
# b = Previous page
# /word = Search forward for 'word'
# n = Next search result
# N = Previous search result
# g = Go to beginning
# G = Go to end

items.title: What is a Package Manager?
items.description: A package manager is like an app store for the command line.
items.code:
# pkg = Termux's package manager (based on apt)

# HOW IT WORKS:
# 1. You tell pkg what you want: pkg install python
# 2. pkg checks what is needed (dependencies)
# 3. pkg downloads from the internet
# 4. pkg installs everything automatically

# WHY THIS IS BETTER:
# - Automatic dependency handling
# - Easy updates for all software
# - Clean uninstallation
# - Verified, safe packages

items.title: Essential pkg Commands
items.description: These are the only pkg commands you need to know.
items.code:
# UPDATE AND UPGRADE (do this first always)
pkg update && pkg upgrade  # Refresh the list of available packages and install newer versions

# SEARCHING
pkg search python    # Find packages related to 'python'
pkg list-all        # Show every available package (very long)

# INSTALLING
pkg install python   # Install Python
pkg install nodejs git vim  # Install multiple packages

# REMOVING
pkg uninstall python  # Remove Python
pkg uninstall python --purge  # Remove including configuration files

# INFORMATION
pkg list-installed   # Show what you have installed
pkg show python      # Show detailed info about a package

# CLEANING (free up space)
pkg clean    # Remove downloaded package files

items.title: Must-Have Packages for Beginners
items.description: Essential packages every Termux user should install.
items.code:
# TEXT EDITORS
pkg install nano     # Simple, beginner-friendly editor
pkg install vim      # Powerful editor (steep learning curve)

# FILE DOWNLOADING
pkg install wget     # Download files from the web
pkg install curl     # Download and test APIs

# PROGRAMMING
pkg install python   # Python 3
pkg install nodejs   # JavaScript runtime
pkg install ruby     # Ruby programming language

# VERSION CONTROL
pkg install git      # Track code changes

# SYSTEM ACCESS
pkg install openssh  # Connect to other computers
pkg install termux-api  # Access Android features

# NETWORK TOOLS
pkg install nmap     # Network scanning
pkg install traceroute  # Trace network path

# UTILITIES
pkg install tree   # Show folder structure as tree
pkg install htop     # Better task manager
pkg install man      # Manual pages

# INSTALL ALL AT ONCE
pkg install nano vim wget curl python nodejs git openssh termux-api tree htop man

items.title: Repository Management
items.description: Packages come from repositories. Sometimes you need to change which repo you use.
items.code:
# DEFAULT REPOSITORIES:
# main - The primary Termux repository
# root - Root-only tools (requires rooted phone)
# x11 - Graphical applications
# game - Games and entertainment

# CHANGE MIRROR (if downloads are slow)
termux-change-repo
# Use arrow keys to select a mirror, Space to select, Enter to confirm

# ADD EXTRA REPOSITORIES
pkg install root-repo   # Root tools (requires root)
pkg install x11-repo    # GUI applications
pkg install game-repo   # Games

# VIEW CURRENT REPOSITORIES
cat $PREFIX/etc/apt/sources.list

items.title: Working with Different Package Sources
items.description: Not all software is in Termux repositories. Here is how to get software from other sources.
items.code:
# PYTHON PACKAGES (pip)
pip install requests
pip install flask
pip list

# NODE.JS PACKAGES (npm)
npm install -g nodemon
npm list -g --depth=0

# RUBY GEMS (gem)
gem install bundler

# BUILDING FROM SOURCE
pkg install build-essential
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git
cd repo
./configure
make
make install

items.title: Setting Up Storage Access
items.description: Before accessing Android files, you must grant permission.
items.code:
# STEP 1: Run the setup command
termux-setup-storage

# STEP 2: Verify it worked
ls ~/storage/
# You should see: shared/ downloads/ dcim/ pictures/ music/ movies/

# TROUBLESHOOTING:
# If storage does not work, try: rm -rf ~/storage && termux-setup-storage

items.title: Accessing Android Folders
items.description: After setup, your Android storage is accessible through ~/storage/.
items.code:
# YOUR PHONE'S STORAGE
~/storage/shared/  # Root of internal storage

# SHORTCUT FOLDERS
~/storage/downloads/  # Browser downloads
~/storage/dcim/  # Camera photos
~/storage/pictures/  # Screenshots and saved images
~/storage/music/    # Music files
~/storage/movies/  # Videos
~/storage/documents/  # Documents folder

# EXAMPLES
ls ~/storage/downloads/
cd ~/storage/dcim/Camera/
cp myfile.txt ~/storage/downloads/

items.title: Moving Files Between Environments
items.description: Termux private storage and Android shared storage are separate.
items.code:
# TERMUX PRIVATE -> ANDROID
cp ~/mynote.txt ~/storage/shared/Documents/

# ANDROID -> TERMUX PRIVATE
cp ~/storage/shared/Download/document.pdf ~/

# COPY WHOLE FOLDERS (use -r for recursive)
cp -r ~/myproject ~/storage/shared/Documents/

# MOVE instead of copy
mv ~/storage/shared/DCIM/old_photo.jpg ~/Pictures/

items.title: Working with External SD Cards
items.description: If your device has an SD card, here is how to access it.
items.code:
# FIND THE SD CARD MOUNT POINT
ls /storage/
# Look for folders like: emulated, sdcard1, or a long alphanumeric string

# COMMON LOCATIONS
/storage/sdcard1/
/storage/XXXX-XXXX/

# CREATE A SYMLINK FOR EASIER ACCESS
ln -s /storage/sdcard1 ~/sdcard

# NOW YOU CAN USE
cd ~/sdcard
ls

# CHECK SD CARD SPACE
df -h | grep sdcard

items.title: Zsh and Oh My Zsh
items.description: Upgrade from Bash to Zsh with themes and plugins.
items.code:
# INSTALL ZSH
pkg install zsh

# CHANGE DEFAULT SHELL
chsh -s zsh

# Restart Termux completely

# INSTALL OH MY ZSH
sh -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"

# CHANGE THEME IN ~/.zshrc
# Find ZSH_THEME="robbyrussell" and change to:
# ZSH_THEME="agnoster"

# Apply changes
source ~/.zshrc

items.title: Color Schemes
items.description: Customize terminal colors by editing colors.properties.
items.code:
# CREATE THE .termux DIRECTORY
mkdir -p ~/.termux

# EDIT COLOR SCHEME
nano ~/.termux/colors.properties

# EXAMPLE THEME:
# background=black
# foreground=white
# cursor=purple

# Apply changes (restart Termux)

items.title: Font Size and Style
items.description: Adjust font size on the fly and change the font family.
items.code:
# FONT SIZE SHORTCUTS (in Termux):
# Volume Up button = Increase font size
# Volume Down button = Decrease font size

# CHANGE FONT FAMILY
nano ~/.termux/termux.properties

# Add or modify this line:
# font = /path/to/font.ttf

# Example using a built-in font:
# font = monospace

# Apply changes
termux-reload-settings

items.title: Custom Aliases
items.description: Create shortcuts for frequently used commands.
items.code:
# EDIT YOUR SHELL CONFIGURATION FILE
nano ~/.zshrc   # If using Zsh
nano ~/.bashrc  # If using Bash

# ADD ALIASES AT THE BOTTOM:
alias ll='ls -la'
alias update='pkg update && pkg upgrade'
alias install='pkg install'
alias remove='pkg uninstall'
alias home='cd ~'

# SAVE AND APPLY
source ~/.zshrc  # or source ~/.bashrc

items.title: Installing Python
items.description: Python is a beginner-friendly programming language.
items.code:
# INSTALLATION
pkg install python

# VERIFY
python --version

# PIP (Python's package manager)
pip --version
pip install --upgrade pip

# RUN PYTHON INTERACTIVE MODE (REPL)
python
>>> print('Hello')
>>> exit()

# RUN A PYTHON SCRIPT
python my_script.py

items.title: Installing Python Packages
items.description: Python has thousands of packages. Use pip to install them.
items.code:
# BASIC COMMANDS
pip install requests
pip install flask numpy pandas
pip uninstall requests
pip list
pip show requests
pip freeze > requirements.txt
pip install -r requirements.txt

# ESSENTIAL PACKAGES
pip install requests    # Download from websites
pip install beautifulsoup4  # Extract data from HTML
pip install flask   # Create web servers
pip install numpy   # Math and arrays
pip install pandas   # Data analysis
pip install matplotlib   # Plotting and graphs

# UPGRADE A PACKAGE
pip install --upgrade requests

# INSTALL SPECIFIC VERSION
pip install requests==2.28.0

items.title: Creating a Simple Web Server
items.description: Start a web server in one line of Python.
items.code:
# METHOD 1: Built-in HTTP server
python -m http.server 8000
# Open http://localhost:8000 in your browser
# Press Ctrl+C to stop

# Serve a specific directory
python -m http.server 8000 --directory /sdcard/Download

# METHOD 2: Flask web framework
pip install flask
# Create app.py and run: python app.py

items.title: Working with APIs
items.description: Fetch data from internet APIs using Python.
items.code:
# INSTALL REQUESTS
pip install requests

# Create a Python script to fetch API data

items.title: Installing Node.js
items.description: Node.js lets you run JavaScript outside of a browser.
items.code:
# INSTALLATION
pkg install nodejs

# VERIFY
node --version
npm --version

# NODE REPL (interactive)
node
> console.log('Hello')
> .exit

# RUN A NODE.JS SCRIPT
node app.js

items.title: Creating a Web Server with Express
items.description: Express is the most popular Node.js web framework.
items.code:
# SETUP
mkdir myapp && cd myapp
npm init -y
npm install express

# Create server.js and run: node server.js

# AUTO-RESTART WITH NODEMON
npm install -g nodemon
nodemon server.js

items.title: Useful npm Packages
items.description: Install global tools and local packages for development.
items.code:
# GLOBAL PACKAGES (use -g flag)
npm install -g nodemon    # Auto-restart server
npm install -g pm2     # Process manager
npm install -g npm-check   # Check outdated packages
npm install -g http-server # Simple HTTP server

# LOCAL PACKAGES (in project directory)
npm install axios   # HTTP client
npm install chalk   # Colored terminal output
npm install dotenv   # Env variables
npm install commander  # Command line interfaces

# USING PM2 (keep running after closing terminal)
pm2 start server.js
pm2 status
pm2 logs
pm2 stop server.js

items.title: Installing and Configuring Git
items.description: Git tracks changes to files. Think of it as an unlimited undo button.
items.code:
# INSTALLATION
pkg install git

# CONFIGURE YOUR IDENTITY (required for commits)
git config --global user.name 'Your Name'
git config --global user.email 'your@email.com'

# VERIFY CONFIGURATION
git config --list

# SET DEFAULT EDITOR
git config --global core.editor 'nano'

# SET LINE ENDINGS
git config --global core.autocrlf input

# ENABLE COLOR
git config --global color.ui auto

items.title: Basic Git Workflow
items.description: This is the standard workflow you will use 90% of the time.
items.code:
# STEP 1: Initialize a repository
git init

# STEP 2: Make changes to your files
# Edit files with nano, create new files, delete files

# STEP 3: Check what changed
git status
git diff

# STEP 4: Stage the changes
git add filename.txt
git add .

# STEP 5: Commit the changes
git commit -m 'Describe what you changed'

# STEP 6: View commit history
git log
git log --oneline
git log --graph --oneline

# STEP 7: Connect to remote repository
git remote add origin https://github.com/username/repo.git

# STEP 8: Push to GitHub
git push -u origin main

# STEP 9: Pull others' changes
git pull origin main

items.title: Understanding Branches
items.description: Branches let you work on different features separately.
items.code:
# CREATE A NEW BRANCH
git branch feature-name
git checkout feature-name
# Or do both at once:
git checkout -b feature-name

# SEE ALL BRANCHES
git branch

# SWITCH BETWEEN BRANCHES
git checkout main

# MERGE A BRANCH
git checkout main
git merge feature-name

# DELETE A BRANCH
git branch -d feature-name

# SEE BRANCH DIFFERENCES
git diff main..feature-name

items.title: Working with GitHub
items.description: Clone repositories and contribute to open source.
items.code:
# CLONE A REPOSITORY (HTTPS)
git clone https://github.com/user/repository.git

# CLONE WITH SSH
git clone git@github.com:user/repository.git

# CLONE SPECIFIC BRANCH
git clone -b branch-name https://github.com/user/repo.git

# FORK WORKFLOW:
# 1. Fork on GitHub website
# 2. Clone your fork
git clone https://github.com/your-username/repo.git
cd repo

# 3. Add original repo as upstream
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original-owner/repo.git

# 4. Fetch upstream changes
git fetch upstream
git merge upstream/main

# 5. Push to your fork
git push origin main

# GENERATE SSH KEY (for GitHub)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C 'your@email.com'
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
# Add this key to GitHub > Settings > SSH and GPG keys

items.title: Undoing Changes
items.description: Fix mistakes and revert changes in Git.
items.code:
# UNSTAGE A FILE (keep changes)
git reset HEAD file.txt

# DISCARD CHANGES TO A FILE
git checkout -- file.txt

# UNDO LAST COMMIT (keep changes)
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# UNDO LAST COMMIT (discard changes)
git reset --hard HEAD~1

# REVERT A COMMIT
git revert COMMIT_HASH

# AMEND LAST COMMIT MESSAGE
git commit --amend -m 'New message'

# ADD FORGOTTEN FILE TO LAST COMMIT
git add forgotten.txt
git commit --amend --no-edit

# RECOVER DELETED COMMIT
git reflog
git checkout COMMIT_HASH

items.title: SSH Client (Connect to Other Computers)
items.description: SSH lets you securely connect to and control other computers.
items.code:
# INSTALLATION
pkg install openssh

# BASIC CONNECTION
ssh username@hostname_or_ip

# EXAMPLE
ssh user@192.168.1.100

# CONNECT WITH CUSTOM PORT
ssh -p 2222 user@server.com

# RUN A SINGLE COMMAND
ssh user@server.com 'ls -la'

# CONNECT WITH PRIVATE KEY
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa user@server.com

# COPY YOUR SSH KEY (for passwordless login)
ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096
ssh-copy-id user@server.com

# SSH CONFIG FILE (for shortcuts)
nano ~/.ssh/config

# Add:
# Host myserver
#     HostName 192.168.1.100
#     User myuser
#     Port 22

# Now connect with: ssh myserver

items.title: SSH Server (Access Your Phone)
items.description: Turn your phone into an SSH server.
items.code:
# START THE SERVER
sshd

# FIND YOUR PHONE'S IP ADDRESS
ifconfig
# or
ip addr show | grep inet

# DEFAULT PORT
# 8022 (not 22 - Android restricts low ports)

# FROM YOUR COMPUTER, CONNECT TO YOUR PHONE
ssh -p 8022 username@phone_ip

# FIND YOUR USERNAME
whoami

# MAKE SSH START AUTOMATICALLY
echo 'sshd' >> ~/.zshrc

# CHECK IF SERVER IS RUNNING
ps aux | grep sshd

# VIEW SSH LOGS
logcat -s Termux:SSH

# CHANGE PASSWORD
passwd

items.title: File Transfer with SCP and Rsync
items.description: SCP and Rsync use SSH to transfer files securely.
items.code:
# COPY FROM PHONE TO COMPUTER
scp -P 8022 username@phone_ip:~/file.txt ./

# COPY FROM COMPUTER TO PHONE
scp -P 8022 localfile.txt username@phone_ip:~/

# COPY FOLDERS (use -r for recursive)
scp -r -P 8022 username@phone_ip:~/myfolder/ ./

# PRESERVE FILE PERMISSIONS
scp -p -P 8022 file.txt username@phone_ip:~/

# USING RSYNC (more efficient)
pkg install rsync

# SYNC FROM PHONE TO COMPUTER
rsync -avz -e 'ssh -p 8022' username@phone_ip:~/documents/ ./backup/

# SYNC FROM COMPUTER TO PHONE
rsync -avz -e 'ssh -p 8022' ./files/ username@phone_ip:~/files/

# RSYNC WITH EXCLUDES
rsync -avz --exclude='*.tmp' --exclude='cache/' -e 'ssh -p 8022' ./data/ user@phone:~/data/

items.title: Network Tools
items.description: Useful commands for testing connectivity.
items.code:
# PING (test connectivity)
ping google.com
ping -c 4 8.8.8.8

# DOWNLOAD FILES
wget https://example.com/file.zip
curl -O https://example.com/file.zip

# DOWNLOAD WITH PROGRESS
wget --progress=bar:force https://example.com/largefile.zip

# CHECK YOUR PUBLIC IP
curl ifconfig.me
curl ipinfo.io/ip

# GET DETAILED IP INFO
curl ipinfo.io

# TEST HTTP HEADERS
curl -I https://google.com

# CHECK OPEN PORTS
pkg install nmap
nmap -p 1-1000 target.com

# TRACE NETWORK ROUTE
traceroute google.com

# DNS LOOKUP
dig google.com
nslookup google.com

items.title: Essential Terminal Shortcuts
items.description: These shortcuts work in almost every terminal environment.
items.code:
Ctrl + C = Stop current process
Ctrl + D = Exit current shell
Ctrl + L = Clear the screen
Ctrl + A = Jump to beginning of line
Ctrl + E = Jump to end of line
Ctrl + U = Delete to beginning of line
Ctrl + K = Delete to end of line
Ctrl + W = Delete previous word
Ctrl + R = Search command history
Up/Down Arrow = Navigate command history
Tab = Auto-complete commands and filenames
Tab Tab (press twice) = Show all completions

items.title: Termux-Specific Shortcuts
items.description: Termux uses volume keys because Android keyboards lack certain keys.
items.code:
Volume Up + Q = Ctrl key
Volume Up + W = Tilde (~)
Volume Up + E = Pipe (|)
Volume Up + R = Backslash (\)
Volume Up + T = Insert key
Volume Up + Y = Scroll up
Volume Up + U = Scroll down
Volume Up + I = Page Up
Volume Up + O = Page Down

Volume Down + D = Escape key
Volume Down + L = Tab key
Volume Down + K = Delete key

# HOW TO USE:
# Press and hold Volume key, tap the letter key, then release both.

# EXTRA KEYS ROW (add permanent shortcut row)
echo 'extra-keys = [["ESC","CTRL","ALT","LEFT","RIGHT","UP","DOWN"]]' > ~/.termux/termux.properties
termux-reload-settings

items.title: Nano Text Editor Shortcuts
items.description: ^ means Ctrl (so ^O means press Ctrl and O together).
items.code:
^O = Save file (Write Out)
^X = Exit Nano
^K = Cut current line
^U = Paste cut line (Uncut)
^W = Search for text (Where is)
^\ = Search and replace
^G = Help (Get Help)
^C = Show cursor position
^_ = Go to line number
^R = Insert another file

Alt + U = Undo
Alt + E = Redo
Alt + A = Select text
Alt + 6 = Copy selected text

# COMMON WORKFLOW:
# 1. Edit your file
# 2. Press ^O to save
# 3. Press Enter to confirm
# 4. Press ^X to exit

# ENABLE LINE NUMBERS
nano ~/.nanorc
echo 'set linenumbers' >> ~/.nanorc

items.title: Vim Text Editor Shortcuts
items.description: Basic Vim commands. Vim has a learning curve but is very powerful.
items.code:
# INSTALL VIM
pkg install vim

# MODES:
# i = Enter Insert mode (edit text)
# Esc = Exit Insert mode back to Normal mode
# v = Enter Visual mode (select text)

# NORMAL MODE COMMANDS:
# :w    = Save file
# :q    = Quit
# :wq   = Save and quit
# :q!   = Quit without saving

# MOVEMENT (Normal mode):
# h = Left, j = Down, k = Up, l = Right
# 0 = Beginning of line
# $ = End of line
# gg = Beginning of file
# G = End of file

# EDITING (Normal mode):
# x = Delete character
# dd = Delete entire line
# yy = Copy (yank) line
# p = Paste below
# P = Paste above

# SEARCH (Normal mode):
# /word = Search forward
# n = Next result
# N = Previous result

# LEARN MORE:
# vimtutor

items.title: Repository Errors
items.description: If 'pkg update' fails, try these solutions.
items.code:
# SOLUTION 1: Change repository mirror
termux-change-repo
# Use arrow keys to select a mirror, Space to select, Enter to confirm

# SOLUTION 2: Clear cache and retry
pkg clean
pkg update

# SOLUTION 3: Reset repositories
rm -rf $PREFIX/var/lib/apt/lists/*
pkg update

# SOLUTION 4: Reinstall keyring
pkg reinstall termux-keyring
pkg update

# SOLUTION 5: Check internet connection
ping google.com

# SOLUTION 6: Check date/time
# Settings > System > Date & time > Automatic

items.title: Storage Permission Issues
items.description: If you cannot access Android files, fix storage permissions.
items.code:
# SOLUTION 1: Re-run storage setup
termux-setup-storage

# SOLUTION 2: Check Android permissions
# Settings > Apps > Termux > Permissions > Allow Storage

# SOLUTION 3: Toggle permission off and on
# 1. Deny storage permission in Android Settings
# 2. Run: termux-setup-storage
# 3. Tap ALLOW when prompted

# SOLUTION 4: Manually recreate symlinks
rm -rf ~/storage
termux-setup-storage

# SOLUTION 5: Android 11+ Scoped Storage
# Settings > Apps > Termux > Permissions > Files and media > Allow management of all files

items.title: Package Installation Failures
items.description: When 'pkg install' fails, use these fixes.
items.code:
# SOLUTION 1: Update everything first
pkg update && pkg upgrade

# SOLUTION 2: Clear cache
pkg clean
apt clean

# SOLUTION 3: Reinstall the package
pkg reinstall <package>

# SOLUTION 4: Check disk space
df -h

# SOLUTION 5: Fix broken dependencies
pkg install -f

# SOLUTION 6: Check if package exists
pkg search <package-name>

items.title: Termux Won't Start or Keeps Crashing
items.description: If Termux crashes on launch, try these solutions.
items.code:
# SOLUTION 1: Clear cache (safe)
# Settings > Apps > Termux > Storage > Clear Cache

# SOLUTION 2: Clear all data (WARNING: deletes everything)
# Settings > Apps > Termux > Storage > Clear Data

# SOLUTION 3: Reinstall Termux
# 1. Backup: cp -r ~ ~/storage/shared/termux-backup/
# 2. Uninstall Termux
# 3. Reinstall from F-Droid
# 4. Restore: cp -r ~/storage/shared/termux-backup/* ~/

# SOLUTION 4: Disable battery optimization
# Settings > Apps > Termux > Battery > Unrestricted

# SOLUTION 5: Check if using correct version
# Make sure you installed from F-Droid, not Play Store

items.title: SSH Connection Issues
items.description: Fix problems when connecting via SSH.
items.code:
# SOLUTION 1: Check if sshd is running
ps aux | grep sshd
# If not running: sshd

# SOLUTION 2: Check correct port (default is 8022)
ssh -p 8022 user@phone_ip

# SOLUTION 3: Find your phone's IP address
ifconfig

# SOLUTION 4: Check if on same network
# Both devices must be on same WiFi network

# SOLUTION 5: Regenerate SSH keys
rm ~/.ssh/ssh_host_*
sshd

# SOLUTION 6: Check permissions
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

# SOLUTION 7: View SSH logs
logcat -s Termux:SSH

items.title: Do I need root access?
items.description: No. Termux works perfectly without root. The vast majority of features work without any root access.

items.title: Play Store vs F-Droid - Which should I use?
items.description: Always use the F-Droid version. The Play Store version is no longer maintained.
items.code:
# F-Droid download: https://f-droid.org/en/packages/com.termux/

# If you already installed from Play Store, uninstall it and install from F-Droid.
# To backup: cp -r ~ ~/storage/shared/termux-backup/

items.title: How do I backup my Termux setup?
items.description: Backup your home directory and list of installed packages.
items.code:
# CREATE BACKUP
tar -czf termux-backup.tar.gz ~/
pkg list-installed > packages.txt
cp termux-backup.tar.gz ~/storage/shared/Documents/
cp packages.txt ~/storage/shared/Documents/

# RESTORE FROM BACKUP
cp ~/storage/shared/Documents/termux-backup.tar.gz ~/
cp ~/storage/shared/Documents/packages.txt ~/
tar -xzf termux-backup.tar.gz -C ~/
xargs pkg install < packages.txt

items.title: How to prevent Android from killing Termux?
items.description: Android may terminate background apps to save battery.
items.code:
# METHOD 1: Disable battery optimization
# Settings > Apps > Termux > Battery > Unrestricted

# METHOD 2: Use wake lock
pkg install termux-api
termux-wake-lock
# To release: termux-wake-unlock

# METHOD 3: Keep in foreground
sleep infinity

# METHOD 4: Lock Termux in recent apps
# Open recent apps, find Termux, tap the lock icon

items.title: Can I run GUI applications?
items.description: Yes, you can run basic GUI applications using VNC or Termux:X11.
items.code:
# METHOD 1: Termux:X11
# 1. Install Termux:X11 from F-Droid
# 2. In Termux:
pkg install x11-repo
pkg install termux-x11

# METHOD 2: VNC Server
pkg install tigervnc
vncserver
# Connect using a VNC viewer app to localhost:5901

# Stop VNC server:
vncserver -kill :1

items.title: How to change the default shell?
items.description: The default shell is Bash. You can switch to Zsh for more features.
items.code:
# INSTALL ZSH
pkg install zsh

# CHANGE DEFAULT SHELL
chsh -s zsh

# Restart Termux

# INSTALL OH MY ZSH (themes and plugins)
sh -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"

# TO CHANGE BACK TO BASH
chsh -s bash

# SEE AVAILABLE SHELLS
cat /etc/shells

items.title: How to update Termux itself?
items.description: Termux is updated through F-Droid, not through pkg.
items.code:
# TO UPDATE TERMUX APP:
# 1. Open F-Droid app
# 2. Go to Settings > Updates
# 3. If Termux has an update, install it

# TO UPDATE PACKAGES INSIDE TERMUX
pkg update && pkg upgrade

# TO CHECK TERMUX VERSION
pkg --version

items.title: How to fix 'Cannot connect to repository' error?
items.description: This usually means your repository mirror is down.
items.code:
# QUICK FIX
termux-change-repo
# Select a different mirror

# IF THAT FAILS
pkg clean
rm -rf $PREFIX/var/lib/apt/lists/*
pkg update

# CHECK INTERNET
ping google.com

# CHECK DATE/TIME
date

items.title: How to access Termux from PC without USB?
items.description: Use SSH to access Termux remotely from your computer over WiFi.
items.code:
# ON YOUR PHONE (Termux)
pkg install openssh
sshd

# Find your phone's IP
ifconfig

# ON YOUR COMPUTER
ssh -p 8022 username@phone_ip

# Transfer files
scp -P 8022 file.txt username@phone_ip:~/

# SET UP PASSWORDLESS LOGIN
# On your computer:
ssh-keygen -t rsa
ssh-copy-id -p 8022 username@phone_ip
